tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'Use double Colorado blades in shallow, cold, and muddy water. Colorado blades give off a ton of vibration helping bass track the bait using its lateral line.'
Hackney: 'Willow leaf blades offer less resistance, so for fishing fast along the surface in clear water, a double willow leaf spinnerbait is a great option.'

Hackney: 'The best all-around spinnerbait is a Colorado/willow combination — smaller Colorado up on the arm, larger willow out on the end. Great for fairly clear to pretty muddy water and temps from 52 to 80+ degrees.'
Hackney: 'Killing a spinnerbait — stopping the retrieve for a second right after it clears cover — lets the blades tumble and flutter. Great way to trigger strikes.'
Hackney: 'Pump or twitch your rod tip during the retrieve for irregular action. Never stop reeling — just intermittently give a quick pump every few seconds.'
Hackney: 'In summer, use a heavier spinnerbait (3/4 oz) to get it down and fish it fast, covering more ground while triggering aggressive warm-water bass.'
Hackney: 'In winter, go with 1/2 or 3/8 oz spinnerbaits. Reeling slower to reach depth gives lethargic winter bass more time to react.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'Round heads are better suited to fishing rock and stumps — less likely to get wedged. Keel-style heads are ideal for fast retrieves and fishing higher in the water column. Bullet heads offer a mix but can get wedged in crevices.'
Hackney: 'Opening the wire spread gives the spinnerbait more lift so you can slow down without cutting vibration. Closing the wire gives more speed and keeps it down.'
Hackney: 'Nine times out of 10 you'll find better success with a spinnerbait fishing around isolated cover like dock posts, stumps, and boulders. Bass position near cover to ambush prey.'
Hackney: 'Split-tail trailers give the most bang for your buck — good action without changing the speed on retrieve or fall. Craw trailers increase profile and slow the bait down.'
Hackney: 'Use a trailer hook slightly smaller than the main hook, oriented the same direction. Run the main hook point through the back of the trailer hook eye.'
Hackney: 'In muddy water, it's more important to increase the size of the blades than to worry about what color they are.'
